title: Torta di castagne
subtitle: Tuscan chestnut flour cake with pine nuts and rosemary
excerpt: Torta di castagne (or castagnaccio) is a thin, dense Tuscan bake made from nothing more than chestnut flour, water, olive oil, and a scattering of pine nuts, raisins, and fresh rosemary. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and vegan. The chestnut flour carries a deep, earthy sweetness; no added sugar is needed. The texture is set but yielding, something between a cake and a thick pancake. Serve in small squares or wedges.
sourceNotes: Castagnaccio is native to Tuscany and Liguria, and records of chestnut flour baking in central Italy date to the sixteenth century. It was historically a peasant food eaten during the chestnut harvest in autumn, when fresh chestnut flour was available. Pellegrino Artusi discusses chestnut preparations in 'La Scienza in cucina' (1891). The version with raisins and pine nuts is the Tuscan standard; Ligurian versions sometimes add walnuts.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. Keeps at room temperature for 1 day; the crust softens after that.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flour made from dried, stone-ground chestnuts. It is naturally sweet, gluten-free, and deeply earthy. It cannot be swapped directly for wheat flour — it has no gluten network and behaves more like a thick batter than a dough. Used in Tuscan castagnaccio, Corsican chestnut polenta, and some northern Italian pasta. Buy from Italian delis or wholefood shops; it deteriorates quickly once opened and should be stored airtight and used within a few months.

content.text: The batter is poured thin; no more than 1.5 cm deep in the tin. A thicker layer stays wet in the centre. The surface should crack slightly as it bakes, and the edges pull away from the tin. Do not overbake: the centre should be just set, not dry.
has a short shelf life once opened; use within 2 months and store in an airtight container.

Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Oil a 20 cm round tin generously. Sift 250 g chestnut flour into a large bowl and add 0.5 tsp salt. Gradually whisk in 400 ml water to make a smooth, pourable batter with no lumps. Stir in 50 ml olive oil. Pour into the prepared tin; the batter should be about 1.5 cm deep. Scatter over 50 g pine nuts, 60 g raisins, and 1 tsp rosemary leaves. Drizzle a thin thread of olive oil across the surface. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the surface has cracked, the edges are set, and the centre is just firm when pressed. Rest for 20 minutes before cutting. Serve at room temperature in wedges.

items.problem: Wet, undercooked centre after the full bake time
items.solution: The batter was poured too deep. The cake should be no more than 1.5 cm thick in the tin. Use a wider tin or reduce the quantity.

items.problem: Batter has lumps that won't beat out
items.solution: Sift the chestnut flour before adding water, and add the water gradually while whisking. Chestnut flour lumps readily in cold liquid; add a little warm water if the lumps persist and whisk vigorously.
This is normal — castagnaccio is dense and moist. It firms slightly as it cools. It should not be dry or crumbly. If it is genuinely wet, the oven temperature was too low or the cake was underbaked.
